g.data-narration: Computer System Architecture defines how hardware components are organized. This determines OS design and system capabilities.
System architecture defines the structure of computing hardware, particularly the number and arrangement of processors in the system.
g.text: Computer system architecture refers to the structure and organization of
computing hardware, particularly with respect to the number and
arrangement of processors. It directly influences OS design.

g.data-narration: A single-processor system has one main CPU executing general-purpose instructions. Most older computers used this design.
Single-processor systems often have special-purpose processors for disks, keyboards, and graphics. These don't run user programs.
g.text: Single-processor systems also have special-purpose processors
for specific devices. They do NOT run user processes.
★ Still classified as single-processor because only ONE general-purpose CPU exists

g.data-narration: Multiprocessor systems have two or more processors sharing bus, clock, memory and devices. This provides increased throughput.
g.text: Two or more processors in close communication, sharing:

g.data-narration: In Asymmetric Multiprocessing, a boss processor controls the system and assigns tasks to worker processors. Simpler but has bottlenecks.
g.text: Boss-Worker model: One processor controls, assigns tasks to others

g.data-narration: In SMP, all processors are peers sharing memory. Each can run OS and user tasks. Modern OS like Windows and Linux use SMP.
g.text: All processors are PEERS - each runs any task including OS functions

g.data-narration: SMP is preferred over AMP in modern systems because there's no bottleneck and graceful degradation when CPUs fail.
g.text: ✓ SMP is the standard in modern systems due to better scalability and fault tolerance

g.data-narration: Multicore puts multiple computing cores on a single chip. Each core has registers and L1 cache, sharing L2 cache and memory.
g.text: Multiple computing cores on a SINGLE chip - more efficient than multiple chips

g.data-narration: NUMA gives each CPU its own local memory for fast access. Remote memory access is slower but possible. OS must be NUMA-aware.
g.text: Addresses bus contention in large SMP by giving each CPU local memory

g.data-narration: Blade servers are modular servers with multiple processing boards in one chassis. Each blade runs independently with its own OS.
g.text: Modular server design with multiple blade processing boards in a single chassis

g.data-narration: Key architectures include single-processor, AMP, SMP, multicore, NUMA, and blade servers. SMP is the modern standard.
Remember these exam tips: SMP vs AMP comparison, three multiprocessor advantages, graceful degradation, and NUMA concepts.
g.text: 1. SMP vs AMP: Know boss-worker vs peer model | 2. Three advantages: throughput, economy, reliability | 3. Graceful degradation: continued operation on failure
